A son of the heavens asked the Buddha, 'Where did this tender, wonderful, and pleasant light come from?' The Buddha replied with silence. The son repeated his question to the Buddha many times. The Buddha finally answered, 'This supremely pure light is from the saha world.'
chapterbody 5: The son asked further, 'The saha world is an evil world consumed by the five defilements. Sentient beings in the saha world have heavy afflictions and an ignorant and obtuse foundation. They are remorseless, shameless, lacking faith, and without reverence. Their karmic hindrances are difficult to eradicate. How can this supremely pure light come from the saha world?'
chapterbody 6: 'It is because, in the saha world, there is a bodhisattva named Padmakumara, who is presently spreading Buddhadharma.'
chapterbody 8: 'What is his background?'
chapterbody 9: 'This Padmakumara emerged from Vairocana Buddha and Buddha Locana. His original abode is Amitabha Buddha's Western Pure Land of Ultimate Bliss.'
chapterbody 11: 'A great many tathagatas have emerged, but none have this light of supreme purity. Why does only Padmakumara have this light?'
chapterbody 12: 'Padmakumara only appears once every 500 years. When I was discoursing the Buddhadharma in the saha world, Padmakumara was Venerable Sariputra.'
chapterbody 13: The sons were amazed and shouted words of praise.
chapterbody 15: The Buddha continued, 'Sariputra had great majestic power, the power of great wisdom, and the power of extreme diligence. He occupied the foremost seat. I bestowed on him the epithet Padmaprabha Buddha.'
chapterbody 16: The celestial sons broke out in applause.
chapterbody 18: The Buddha added, 'When Padmakumara expounds the Dharma in the saha world, Mahabrahma and Indra frequently attend his discourses, and so do many bodhisattvas and shravakas.
chapterbody 19: 'He has omniscient knowledge of Hinayana, Mahayana, Vajrayana, and all the doctrines of Buddhism. He enters countless samadhis, is completely unhindered in Buddhadharma, attains dharanis, and is endowed with incredible merit. He gives countless discourses for the purpose of guiding sentient beings to Buddhahood.
chapterbody 20: 'He radiates the light of supreme purity which attracts all sentient beings to form an affinity with him. This is why he radiates supremely pure light.'
chapterbody 22: The celestial sons responded, 'We would all like to protect and support him. Can we do this?'
chapterbody 23: The Buddha replied, 'Padmakumara already has many bodhisattvas and shravakas with great merit and affinity to assist him. Moreover, there are many devotees with affinity from past lifetimes who are following him. Additionally, there are countless attendants with virtuous roots listening to him expound the Dharma. A Padmakumara does not often appear.'
chapterbody 25: The Buddha continued, 'Go!'
chapterbody 26: Therefore, I (Grandmaster Lu) obtained the support of celestial beings!

chaptername: 033 How Pure Light Emerges
chapterbody: randmaster Lu says: Cultivating inner fire is, of course, a method to cause pure light to appear, since inner fire is the combination of light and heat.
chapterbody 3: Cultivating light drops is another method, since this practice opens the five chakras. Pure light then emerges. For example, when the heart chakra opens, a primordial pure light will emerge and naturally shine from the chakra.
chapterbody 5: Another method of causing pure light to appear is to cultivate Sleeping-in-Light Practice. One falls asleep while visualizing the light of one's light drops in the five chakras, and sleeps in the midst of this light.
chapterbody 7: For example:
chapterbody 8: Heart chakra—blue light drops.
chapterbody 9: Throat chakra—blue light drops.
chapterbody 10: Brow chakra—red light drops.
chapterbody 11: Secret chakra—black light drops.
chapterbody 15: If one sleeps in the midst of light, one will not suddenly wake up during the night.
chapterbody 16: Will not be deluded.
chapterbody 17: Will not forget or lose bodhicitta.
chapterbody 18: Will not suffer insomnia.
chapterbody 19: Will not be tired.
chapterbody 20: Will not have nightmares.
chapterbody 21: (The most important key cultivation formula of the Sleeping-in-Light Practice is to visualize the syllables in the chakras radiating pure light, and sleeping in pure-light samadhi.)
chapterbody 23: In the Sleeping-in-Light Practice—
chapterbody 24: Practitioner is able to transform into the personal deity.
chapterbody 25: Practitioner can manifest in the palace of the personal deity.
chapterbody 26: Practitioner can transform into an infinite number of practitioner's personal deity and transform an infinite number of practitioner's personal deity back into one.
chapterbody 28: Also in dreams—
chapterbody 29: Practitioner can travel to any pure land.
chapterbody 30: Practitioner can transform any sentient being into practitioner's retinue.
chapterbody 31: Practitioner can enter samadhi.
chapterbody 32: These all occur in the midst of light. In other words, the world transforms into light, all sentient beings transform into light, and the personal deity transforms into light. Everything transforms into light and abides in the midst of light.
chapterbody 34: Grandmaster Lu says:
chapterbody 35: Primordial pure light—emerges during Sleeping-in-Light Practice or during annihilation. It is the true and original light. (Extremely important)
chapterbody 36: Primordial enlightenment light—emerges when one is completely enlightened in all dharmas. It is a natural and impartial light and the light of emptiness. It can be produced during the daytime when one cultivates, at night when one is sleeping, or when one is dying. (Key cultivation formula: it appears in the four states of emptiness)
chapterbody 37: Primordial attainment light—emerges upon attainment of the unification of the mother pure light and child pure light.
chapterbody 39: I explain the key cultivation formulas as follows:
chapterbody 40: Visualize oneself transforming into the personal deity. A four-petaled lotus appears in the heart of the personal deity. Syllables on the petals respectively emit light.
chapterbody 41: In the center of the lotus, there is a 'hum' syllable emitting blue light. On the front petal, there is a 'ah' syllable emitting white light. On the right petal, there is a 'lu' syllable emitting yellow light. On the back petal, there is a 'da' syllable emitting red light. On the left petal, there is a 'la' syllable emitting green light. (complete attainment of the four levels of emptiness)
chapterbody 42: The light emitted by these five syllables is differentiated as follows: The wisdom light of ultimate reality (Hum). The wisdom light of the Great Perfect Mirror (Ah). The wisdom light of equanimity (Lu). The wisdom light of discriminatory awareness (Da). The wisdom light of accomplishing all (La).
chapterbody 45: Concerning the pure light practice, there are many key cultivation formulas. I have outlined the most important ones. For more details, one must seek verbal transmission.

chaptername: 034 Key Cultivation Formula for Bardo Deliverance Pith Teachings on the Path of Liberation
chapterbody: randmaster Lu says: The bardo is an intermediate state. A being in the bardo is commonly called a spirit.
chapterbody 2: Although I am not yet dead, I frequently cultivate the Bardo Consciousness Transference Practice. Every person will die. Therefore, practicing for the transition of death is extremely important.
chapterbody 4: Firstly, in the process of dying, at the first moment of death, if one is able to maintain the bright light of consciousness, one accomplishes the bright Dharma body [dharmakaya]. If one is unable to maintain the bright light of consciousness, but relies on the power of the self-development stage to attain the illusory body of the personal deity, one accomplishes the bright reward body [sambhogakaya]. If one is unable to attain either the bright Dharma body or the reward body, one's last choice is to cultivate an illusory emanation body through the womb. This is the accomplishment of a bright emanation body [nirmanakaya]. (This is the difference between the three bodies of dharmakaya, sambhogakaya, and nirmanakaya)
chapterbody 8: Eight signs of impending death:
chapterbody 9: Earth dissolving into water—physical body cannot move, one cannot support oneself, the feeling of a landslide, the earth splitting, heaviness, and being torn apart.
chapterbody 10: Water dissolving into fire—sweat and saliva dry up.
chapterbody 11: Fire dissolving into wind—one's body becomes cold, loss of bodily warmth.
chapterbody 12: Wind dissolving into consciousness—one's outbreaths exceed one's inbreaths, breathing stops.
chapterbody 13: Consciousness dissolving into Appearance—bright and clear moonlight appears in a cloudless sky.
chapterbody 14: Appearance dissolving into Increase—red-yellow light appears like the sun rising in a cloudless sky. Increase dissolving into
chapterbody 15: Attainment—dusk-like darkness appears in a cloudless sky.
chapterbody 16: Disappearance of illusory states—
chapterbody 20: I say: Those who attain the rainbow light will not need to experience the bardo. Those who attain the Formless Realm will also not experience the bardo. Those who attain the Form Realm and the Desire Realm must experience the bardo.
chapterbody 24: The bardo spirit has six characteristics:
chapterbody 28: Phenomena of a bardo spirit entering a womb:
chapterbody 29: When a bardo spirit sees males and females engage in sexual activity.
chapterbody 30: When sexual desire arises in the mind of a bardo spirit.
chapterbody 31: When a bardo spirit gradually faints and loses consciousness.
chapterbody 32: Birth by transformation, moisture-born, viviparity, oviparity, and birth in the three evil realms are all like this.
chapterbody 33: bright light appears in a clear sky at dawn, without moon, sun, dusk, or darkness. This is the true light, which is of utmost importance.
chapterbody 36: Rebirth in the evil realms may occur in the following three ways:
chapterbody 37: The bardo spirit will be reborn in the animal realm if entering a cave, brush, a forest, etc.
chapterbody 38: The bardo spirit will be reborn in the realm of hungry ghosts if entering a run down shed on a dark and rainy night.
chapterbody 39: The bardo spirit will be reborn in hell if entering an iron room.
chapterbody 43: Grandmaster Lu says: Many phenomena may occur in the bardo. One must consult a great virtuous adept about them.
chapterbody 45: Unhindered travel anywhere in an instant.
chapterbody 49: Able to see spirits calculating the good and evil of the bardo spirit.
chapterbody 51: Able to see food and drink, but only able to consume the qi.
chapterbody 53: Can do anything, invisible to most people.
chapterbody 54: Can observe what people are doing.
chapterbody 55: No sun, no moon, no stars.

chaptername: 035 My Bardo Experience
chapterbody: (Grandmaster Lu) use Machig Labdron's 'severing practice' (Chod Practice) to practice death. I also use the Corpse Pose Practice [Shavasana]. (Please note: I have transmitted the Machig Labdron Personal Deity Practice and the Black Wrathful Dakini Practice)
chapterbody 2: In the Machig Labdron and Black Wrathful Dakini practices, the practitioner performs the practice of offering their body to the Four Holy Realms, the Six Realms, and the Ten Dharma Realms.
chapterbody 4: The method of cultivation is as follows:
chapterbody 5: The Black Wrathful Dakini is transformed from a 'pei' syllable. The practitioner transforms the 'pei' syllable to be as enormous as Mt. Sumeru. The Black Wrathful Dakini severs practitioner's head, and cuts practitioner's body into pieces. Arrange the bones into a tripod. Place the crown of the skull (kapala) on top of the tripod. Practitioner's head, four limbs, and torso are placed inside the skull. An 'ah' syllable underneath the tripod bursts into fire. Practitioner melts, boils, and transforms into nectar. (A white-colored 'han' syllable transforms into nectar.)
chapterbody 6: The nectar is offered upward to the Four Holy Realms (Buddhas, bodhisattvas, arhats, and pratyekabuddhas), and downward to the Six Realms (Heaven, Human, Asura, Hell, Hungry Ghost, Animal).
chapterbody 7: As a result of this vast body offering, practitioner's karmic creditors, life or monetary debt collectors, karmic retribution, maras of illness, and devils will all be repaid. They will no longer create obstacles to harm the practitioner, who by this offering, is liberated.
chapterbody 8: In terms of the absolute aspect, I have already died, no-self, no afflictions, and no grasping. All the precepts I have violated are purified through this repentance.
chapterbody 9: Practitioner then continues to pray to the guru and personal deity for their great blessings, transformation into the illusory body (bardo spirit), and attainment of liberation without obstacles. (Corpse Pose Practice and Machig Labdron Practice have identical meaning and significance.)
chapterbody 13: Remember the key cultivation formula.
chapterbody 14: The light of the Dharma body.
chapterbody 15: The light of the reward body.
chapterbody 16: The light of the emanation body.
chapterbody 17: (Whether prior to taking birth or after death, one must always keep the Brilliant Light Practice in mind. In this way, one will be able to control one's rebirth.)
chapterbody 18: In my opinion, the ability to enter samadhi is of critical importance because in the equanimity of indivisible bliss and emptiness, one merges with the light.
chapterbody 22: While in the bardo, one must sever 'greed'. This includes excessive desire for:
chapterbody 23: Gold, silver, jewels, money.
chapterbody 24: Houses, cars, land.
chapterbody 25: Those one loves the most.
chapterbody 26: Those one hates the most.
chapterbody 27: Family members, friends, benefactors, and all one's relatives.
chapterbody 28: Blissful sensations arising from cultivation.
chapterbody 29: And so forth.
chapterbody 33: While in the bardo, one must have the power of meditation, because one is only able to merge with brilliant light while in samadhi. Merge with Dharma body. Merge with reward body. Merge with emanation body.
chapterbody 34: My personal experience is, whether prior to birth or after death, whether awake or dead, one must remain in samadhi at all times. In this way, one can let go, be unhindered, and attain liberation.

chaptername: 036 Explanation By an Example
chapterbody: lady boarded a long distance flight with her husband. During the flight, while the husband was sleeping, the lady went to use the restroom. When the husband awoke after a long sleep, he realized that his wife was away from her seat and had been gone for a very long time. The husband searched everywhere for his wife, but couldn't find her anywhere.
chapterbody 3: The husband thought she was probably in the restroom, so he looked in all the restrooms. There was one restroom which had been locked for a long time without anyone going in or out. The husband said to the flight attendant, 'My wife is in there!'
chapterbody 4: The flight attendant asked, 'Are you sure?'
chapterbody 5: The husband replied, 'Definitely. I have looked everywhere else!'
chapterbody 6: The flight attendant tried to figure out how to open the restroom door.
chapterbody 7: When she got it open, 'Wow! It turned out that the wife had fainted and died inside!'
chapterbody 8: The captain then made an announcement asking any doctor on board to render emergency aid. Afterwards, the plane landed in Taiwan. The wife's body was then transported back to the couple's own country.
chapterbody 12: According to what I know, the lady fainted after entering the restroom. Her bardo spirit having departed from the body made every effort to return but failed. She totally panicked. Her soul was able to go through the walls and see her husband searching for her. She was following her husband by his side, but he couldn't see her.
chapterbody 13: Since then, the couple has been separated forever. The wife died, leaving her husband behind full of regret and sorrow!
chapterbody 17: This couple were my (Grandmaster Lu's) disciples. Afterwards, I was asked to perform bardo deliverance. By this example, I would like to specially teach everyone that, when a soul departs, the only thing the soul should be mindful of is the guru, the personal deity, and the Dharma protector.
chapterbody 18: At this time, one must realize that one is in the bardo, and the Vajrayana practitioner should only be mindful of the three roots. By coupling one's own power developed through cultivation with the power of the guru, the personal deity, and the Dharma protector, a body of light will manifest to guide one to the pure land.
chapterbody 20: Practitioners must refrain from thinking of their husband, children, relatives, home, wealth, or favorite jewelry (diamonds, pearls).
chapterbody 21: If unable to let go of this mundane world, one will fall into the realm of sentient beings. If practitioner can be mindful of the guru, personal deity, or Dharma protector, and furthermore, single-mindedly recite their heart mantras, the practitioner will be delivered and liberated.
chapterbody 22: Grandmaster Lu wants everyone to know that one should not grasp at the illusory forms of the mundane world. Instead, one should stay focused and concentrate on praying for rebirth in the pure land. This is the most important key cultivation formula.

chaptername: 037 About the Bardo Spirit
chapterbody: fter a person dies, the bardo spirit manifests. At this time, one must save oneself (Bardo Deliverance). The bardo spirit is immaterial and therefore free from obstacles. At the onset of the bardo, most people will suddenly be lost in a dreamlike state. Their reactions will follow the environment. For this reason, Vajrayana practitioners must cultivate dreams by being awake and lucid while dreaming. They must also cultivate illusions by being aware of illusion in the midst of illusion.
chapterbody 3: When the bardo begins, one will sometimes hear terrifying sounds such as miserable crying, roaring screams, gloomy and cold shrieking, or howling curses. (The practitioner must understand that these sounds are dreamlike and illusory. There is no need to be afraid.)
chapterbody 4: Or, one may experience being burnt by wild flames with no way to escape; drowning and unable to breathe; falling off a cliff and being smashed into pieces; stabbed to death and screaming in pain; chased and bitten by wild animals with no place to escape; ghosts demanding repayment of debts and forced into a dead end by ghosts; crushed into meat paste by boulders crashing down from a mountain; journeying through a roaring desert of howling wind...
chapterbody 5: One may also encounter being eaten by evil spirits or powerful ghosts, judgment by Lord Yama, and so forth.
chapterbody 7: When these phenomena manifest, the Vajrayana practitioner must remember they are only dreams and illusions. They are without self-nature, totally false, and nothing to be afraid of. At this very moment, maintain meditative stability and mindfulness of the guru, the personal deity, or the Dharma protector. One may recite the mantra of the guru, the personal deity, the Dharma protector, or buddha's epithet to emit clear light. One then merges one's own clear light with the primordial clear light. This is the attainment of liberation.
chapterbody 11: As practitioners, we must understand that to initiate the bardo spirit, one must find an exit for the spirit. The bardo spirit of a Vajrayana practitioner should only exit through the crown aperture (Phowa).
chapterbody 12: Exit through the brow chakra—heaven realm.
chapterbody 13: Exit through the eyes—rebirth as a human.
chapterbody 14: Exit through the ears—non-human path (ghost).
chapterbody 15: Exit through the nostrils—rebirth as a Yaksha.
chapterbody 16: Exit through the mouth—rebirth as a hungry ghost.
chapterbody 17: Exit through the navel chakra—rebirth in the Desire Heaven.
chapterbody 18: Exit through the secret chakra—rebirth as an animal.
chapterbody 19: Exit through the anus—rebirth in hell.
chapterbody 21: I, Grandmaster Lu, suggest that one should have capable practitioners block the eight gates of samsara with syllables. Originally, each opening should be blocked with its respective syllable, which is difficult to visualize. I therefore say that every opening can be blocked with a 'hum' syllable or clear light. However, this blocking should only be performed by one who is capable.

chaptername: 038 How to Discern One's Death Is Approaching
chapterbody: randmaster Lu says that one must not commit suicide because killing oneself is equivalent to killing a buddha. Why is suicide killing a buddha? Because all sentient beings have Buddha-nature and are future buddhas. If one commits suicide, one commits the sin of killing the wisdom life of a buddha, and one will fall into hell.
chapterbody 2: Also, since one's time of death has not yet arrived, one cannot be reborn in the pure land but becomes a fierce and harmful ghost instead.
chapterbody 6: If one perceives omens of approaching death, one can take certain measures. For example: Releasing captive animals. Giving alms. Praying for the blessings of the guru and personal deity. Cultivating longevity practices.
chapterbody 7: There are many more practices one may perform to extend one's life: Reciting the profound sutras. Reciting Vajrayana mantras. Visualizing the power of the guru or of the personal deity. Prostrating before a stupa, or circumambulating stupas, temples, or sacred ground. Praising the merit of buddhas and bodhisattvas. Understanding true emptiness. Practicing the Prolonging Life by Merging with Emptiness Method. By the power of precepts, meditation, and wisdom. By the blessing of the Three Jewels, Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.
chapterbody 8: Entering samadhi. And so forth.
chapterbody 12: Observe the signs of approaching death:
chapterbody 13: Under an immaculate, empty sky without clouds, stand with both arms stretched out to the side and look down to see one's own shadow. If one sees only one's hands or feet, this is a sign that death is approaching.
chapterbody 14: (Very mysterious) Frequently seeing those who have already died in one's dreams is an omen of approaching death. Dreaming that a deceased person is taking one on a tour of a flower garden. (All the flowers are red) Dreaming of places one has never been before. Dreaming that one is solitary and alone in an open place. Dreaming that one is riding a donkey heading south. Dreaming that one is picking red flowers. Dreaming that one is fighting a ghost. Dreaming of Lord Yama (the Lord of Death) And so forth.
chapterbody 18: Press hands against both ears, one cannot hear a thing. When opening eyes, one sees a vast expanse of white. One's temper suddenly and drastically changes. Suddenly, love transforms into hate, hate transforms into love.
chapterbody 19: A serious illness takes a sudden turn for the better, terminal lucidity. Ears lose sense of hearing. Tongue loses sense of taste. Eyes can no longer see anything. Body loses sense of touch. Loss of essence and blood. Tongue becomes dry and turns black. Collapse of the nasal alae. The four elements diminish. (These are all possible signs that death is approaching.)
chapterbody 23: For a Vajrayana practitioner, repentance and restoration of purity are the key points to avoid premature death or accidents. Perform offering to the guru, personal deity, and Dharma protector. Offer goods to the Three Jewels. Offer tormas to the Dharma protector. Cultivate the wheel of a feast offering. Diligently cultivate yoga until one attains spiritual union. One will then stay healthy and extend one's lifespan. Practitioners must also pay attention to blood pressure, blood sugar, blood lipids, cholesterol and so forth.

chaptername: 039 Key Cultivation Formulas of Opening the Crown Aperture (Phowa)
chapterbody: randmaster Lu says: In my book, The Hevajra Tantra-Emptiness Within Great Bliss, I discussed the key cultivation formulas of Non-leakage Practice. One method of opening the crown aperture (phowa) is to lift one's qi from the sacral chakra to the crown chakra. In addition, in our cultivation of Treasure Vase Breathing, the final 'shooting breath', shooting a breath directly to the crown, is another method of opening the crown aperture (phowa).
chapterbody 5: The key cultivation formula of opening the crown aperture (phowa) is:
chapterbody 6: First, visualize the three channels and seven chakras within one's body. Next, visualize a blue 'hum' syllable in one's heart emitting light. Cultivate Treasure Vase Breathing. Lift the Lower-body Qi upwards. Fiercely recite the 'seh' syllable, the 'hum' syllable, or the 'han' syllable 21 times. (While reciting the syllable and shooting the qi up to the crown, raise both hands up high.) When one's qi rises up and collides with the crown aperture, one may visualize that the hum syllable ascends and enters directly into the heart of the guru. Practice Treasure Vase Breathing again. Recite the 'ka' syllable 21 times. Press the Upper-body Qi downwards. This is namely the 'hum' syllable descending to the heart chakra.
chapterbody 10: What's described above is: One lifts the 'hum' qi up. One presses the 'hum' qi down. This lifting qi up and pressing qi down is the method of opening the crown aperture (phowa).
chapterbody 11: As for the syllable recited while one lifts the qi, my guru mentioned the following different syllables at different times; the 'hum' syllable, the 'hei' syllable, the 'seh' syllable, the 'han' syllable, and the 'ah' syllable.
chapterbody 12: (Both hands make a fist while one raises one's hands up.) However, the syllable to be recited while one is pressing the qi down is always 'ka'. This 'ka' syllable is the 'ka' of 'Karmapa', and the 'ka' of 'Kagyu sect', which sounds like 'ka' or 'ka ah'.
chapterbody 16: When the bardo spirit exits from the crown aperture, there are three levels:
chapterbody 17: 1. Rebirth of the emanation body in the pure land:
chapterbody 18: Inhale as much air as possible through both nostrils, recite the 'seh' syllable 21 times, and recite the 'seh' fiercely on the final recitation. The bardo spirit shoots out through the crown aperture and reaches the pure land. This is the rebirth of the emanation body in the pure land.
chapterbody 19: 2. Rebirth of the reward body in the pure land:
chapterbody 20: First visualize Guru Vajra Holder atop the crown, inhale as much as possible through both nostrils, recite the 'seh' syllable 21 times, and as previously described, recite the 'seh' fiercely on the final recitation. The bardo spirit shoots out through the crown aperture and resides in the heart of Guru Vajra Holder. This is the rebirth of the reward body in the pure land.
chapterbody 21: 3. Rebirth of the Dharma body in the pure land:
chapterbody 22: As previously described, inhale through both nostrils, recite the 'seh' syllable 21 times, and recite the 'seh' fiercely on the final recitation. The bardo spirit shoots out through the crown aperture, transforms into emptiness, and merges with emptiness. This is the rebirth of the Dharma body in the pure land.
chapterbody 23: These three types of rebirth are different due to their rebirth in the 'pure land', 'Vajra Holder', and 'emptiness'.

chaptername: 040 Cessation and Visualization
chapterbody: omeone asked Grandmaster Lu, 'What is cessation? How is cessation accomplished?' I answered: Body cessation—meditate in the lotus position. Speech cessation—stay silent. Mind cessation—don't think of the past; don't fabricate thoughts of the future; don't discriminate, let the mind rest in uncontrived equanimity.
chapterbody 3: When one practices cessation, the first key cultivation formula is single-minded concentration.
chapterbody 4: How does one deal with the rising of myriad thoughts?
chapterbody 5: 1. Focus on an object. This is 'cessation by reliance'.
chapterbody 6: For example: Light a stick of incense, focus on the glowing red tip of the incense, stop thoughts from arising, continue in this state without interruption. Or, one may visualize the personal deity, a dharma implement, a mantra syllable... This is a method by which one trains in cessation.
chapterbody 7: 2. Cessation without reliance.
chapterbody 8: Rest in naturalness.
chapterbody 9: Visualize self-nature.
chapterbody 10: Generally speaking, when training in cessation, two types of phenomena will occur. The first phenomenon is 'restlessness'. One's thoughts whirl wildly and are completely unstoppable. At this time, one must relax body and mind with eyes looking down and sitting in a restful position. One's body and mind will then enter into naturalness.
chapterbody 11: The other phenomenon is 'drowsiness'. Many people just fall asleep when practicing cessation, and may even snore. At this time, sit up straight, look up, refresh oneself, and pull oneself together.
chapterbody 12: The key cultivation formula of the cessation practice:
chapterbody 13: When one's qi is calm, the mind is also tranquil. When qi is chaotic, the mind will also be chaotic. Therefore, if one cultivates one's qi to naturalness, the mind will also rest peacefully. An 'undistracted mind' is a sign of accomplishment.
chapterbody 17: Someone asked Grandmaster Lu, 'What is visualization? How does one cultivate visualization?'
chapterbody 18: I answered:
chapterbody 19: Visualization is a kind of wisdom. Through this wisdom, one understands that the eyes, ears, nose, tongue, body, consciousness, and in fact all phenomena are totally without self-nature.
chapterbody 20: I am not I.
chapterbody 21: Thoughts are not thoughts.
chapterbody 22: All the myriad phenomena are unreal.
chapterbody 23: They are like dreams and illusions. Even so-called dreams and illusions are fake names and unattainable.
chapterbody 24: Think about the life of a human being, birth, old age, sickness, and death, a hundred-year dream. Nothing whatsoever can be gained. Therefore, there is neither victory nor evil, no discrimination, and no grasping. Be omnipresent, abiding in tranquility.
chapterbody 25: Come to the firm belief that this saha world is just a play of illusory people in an illusory environment resulting in illusory happenings and substances. Nothing in the past can be gained. Nothing in the present can be gained. Nothing in the future can be gained. Because of this observation, Vajrayana practitioners do not grasp at anything, just abide in peace.
chapterbody 27: I believe:
chapterbody 28: Observe the meaning of emptiness.
chapterbody 29: Understand the meaning of emptiness.
chapterbody 30: Realize the meaning of emptiness.
chapterbody 31: In other words, when one's body, speech, and mind are empty, one reaches a great emptiness.
chapterbody 32: If distracted or wandering thoughts arise, one dispels them by saying 'pei'!

chaptername: 041 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (1)
chapterbody: uestion: 'Grandmaster Lu, what trends do you see in the future of True Buddha School?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'I do not think of the present, nor do I think of the future. Trending or not trending does not exist.'
chapterbody 4: Question: 'You wrote Pith Teachings to the Path of Liberation. What is the true and authentic key cultivation formula?'
chapterbody 5: Answer: 'Liberation is just a name. True liberation is knowing one does not need liberation.' 'We do not understand this.'
chapterbody 6: 'Because all dharmas are illusory.'
chapterbody 7: 'We feel bound in every way. How can you say all dharmas are illusory?'
chapterbody 8: 'One is in bondage because one has tied oneself up. You are bound by your own greedy thoughts. In the absence of greedy thoughts, how are you bound? Upon understanding there is nothing to gain, you are no longer bound.'
chapterbody 10: Question: 'Who is a person of great virtue and knowledge?'
chapterbody 11: Answer: 'Grandmaster Lu.'
chapterbody 12: Question: 'Isn't this blowing your own horn?'
chapterbody 13: Answer: 'Not at all! Anyone who reaches the state of total and complete realization can call themselves Grandmaster Lu. Grandmaster Lu is not a person. For example, Vairocana Buddha is Grandmaster Lu, Buddha Locana is Grandmaster Lu, Amitabha Buddha is Grandmaster Lu, Padmakumara is Grandmaster Lu''
chapterbody 14: 'This is according to what theory?'
chapterbody 15: 'The theory of absolute truth. Because there is no Grandmaster Lu, everyone is Grandmaster Lu.'
chapterbody 17: Question: 'Is it important to have reverence for and faith in Grandmaster Lu?'
chapterbody 18: Answer: 'Only if one has firm and unshakeable faith in Grandmaster Lu, can one attain spiritual union in Guru Yoga. Otherwise, there will never be spiritual union.'
chapterbody 20: Question: 'What if one follows another guru?'
chapterbody 21: Answer: 'That is one's affinity. It is fine for one to follow another guru as long as the guru is highly virtuous and knowledgeable. One must not, however, follow evil sects and crooked paths.'
chapterbody 23: 'How does one recognize evil sects and crooked paths?'
chapterbody 24: 'By observation. A true and authentic guru practices equality without discrimination, has long detached from the eight worldly concerns, and has eradicated greed, anger, and ignorance.'
chapterbody 26: 'If one were to follow Grandmaster Lu and then later follow someone else, what would you think about it?'
chapterbody 27: 'This happens naturally because of insufficient affinity.'
chapterbody 29: 'Grandmaster Lu is the reincarnation of Sariputra from the era of Shakyamuni Buddha. Does Grandmaster remember this past lifetime?'
chapterbody 30: 'Yes, I do. At that time, I held Shakyamuni Buddha and Manjusri Bodhisattva in great reverence. The merit of Manjusri Bodhisattva is truly amazing and incredible, not to mention Shakyamuni Buddha.'

chaptername: 042 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (2)
chapterbody: uestion: 'What great merit does Manjusri Bodhisattva have?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'Shakyamuni Buddha told Sariputra that, even expending a kalpa, Manjusri Bodhisattva's merit couldn't be completely described.'
chapterbody 4: Question: 'Would you describe some of it?'
chapterbody 5: Answer: 'Shakyamuni Buddha said that an infinite number of buddhas entered nirvana because Manjusri Bodhisattva enlightened them to supreme bodhi.'
chapterbody 7: 'Anything else?'
chapterbody 8: 'The mighty divine power of Manjusri Bodhisattva is unlimited. Manjusri blessed the Mara, and caused the Mara to transform into the Buddha sitting on the lion throne discoursing the Buddhadharma. This is something truly extraordinary and seldom seen. His mighty divine power is therefore unparalleled.'
chapterbody 10: 'Manjusri does indeed have mighty divine power. Is there anything more?'
chapterbody 11: 'Once, seven days of heavy rain flooded the Bamboo Grove. The bhikkhus had nothing to eat. Those bhikkhus who were able to enter samadhi entered samadhi. Those bhikkhus who were unable to enter samadhi starved to the point they could no longer move. Luckily, they had Manjusri Bodhisattva who traveled far away begging with a bowl for food. This bowl of food was able to satisfy the hunger of 500 bhikkhus without needing replenishment. With just this one small bowl, Manjusri was able to feed the entire country and still have leftovers.'
chapterbody 13: 'This was a great divine transformation. What else?'
chapterbody 14: 'Manjusri Bodhisattva was foremost in wisdom, beyond compare. Although Sariputra also had great wisdom, his wisdom was miniscule in comparison to Manjusri Bodhisattva.'
chapterbody 16: 'Can you give us an example?'
chapterbody 17: 'Someone asked Manjusri, 'when did you attain Buddhahood?' Manjusri replied 'I, Manjusri, am bodhi. Bodhi is none other than Manjusri. Manjusri is the emptiness.'
chapterbody 19: 'Why did Manjusri say this?'
chapterbody 20: 'Manjusri Bodhisattva was non-arising, non-doing, and non-dual. That is why he said this.'
chapterbody 21: Note:
chapterbody 22: Non-arising—therefore omnipresent.
chapterbody 23: Non-doing—carrying out activities to benefit sentient beings is equivalent to not carrying out any activities.
chapterbody 24: Non-dual—Manjusri is buddha. Buddha is Manjusri.
chapterbody 25: (This is the absolute truth.)
chapterbody 27: Question: 'How is Manjusri Bodhisattva omnipresent in the ten directions, three times, and all Dharma realms?'
chapterbody 28: Answer: 'One time during the summer retreat, Mahakyasapa asked Manjusri where his retreat was. Manjusri responded that it was at the place of Ajatashatru's Queen. Mahakyasapa was furious. He assembled all the bhikkhus for the purpose of banishing Manjusri from the sangha. Shakyamuni Buddha asked Mahakyasapa to look up at the sky. There were countless Manjusris throughout the ten directions, three times, and all Dharma realms. Shakyamuni asked Mahakyasapa, which Manjusri do you want to banish from the sangha?'
chapterbody 30: 'The Buddha spoke about the Three Liberations. Why did Manjusri Bodhisattva never speak about the Three Liberations?'
chapterbody 31: 'The Three Liberations which the Buddha spoke of were the skillful and expedient means of the tathagata, undertaken for the benefit of sentient beings. Manjusri's absolute truth is fearless and hence does not require liberation. Only those with fear need to be liberated.'
chapterbody 33: 'Is your book Pith Teachings on the Path of Liberation an expedient
chapterbody 34: means?'
chapterbody 35: 'Yes it is. Yes it is.'

chaptername: 043 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (3)
chapterbody: uestion: 'Grandmaster Lu, do you dream while you sleep?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'Yes. I am able to enter and exit the dream state. While in the dream state, I deliver sentient beings, cultivate, recite mantras, and benefit sentient beings.'
chapterbody 4: Question: 'Does Grandmaster Lu enter the dreams of his disciples?'
chapterbody 5: 'I am able to, in my disciple's dream, transmit mantras, resolve doubts and problems, and cure illness, which is altogether quite miraculous.'
chapterbody 7: 'How are you able to accomplish this?'
chapterbody 8: 'Secretly practicing Dream Yoga.'
chapterbody 9: Note:
chapterbody 10: Entering a dream—entering a disciple's dream.
chapterbody 11: Exiting a dream—exiting a disciple's dream.
chapterbody 12: Vivid dreams—everything is crystal clear.
chapterbody 13: Transforming dreams—changing a dream which is a karmic obstacle into a pure dream.
chapterbody 14: Cultivating in a dream—ability to cultivate in a dream.
chapterbody 15: Perceiving in dreams—able to see the Path in a dream, seeing the Buddha in a dream.
chapterbody 17: Question: 'How can one tell it's not an illusion created by the devil?'
chapterbody 18: Answer: 'If the guru appears in one's dream and disappears immediately after one recites the Guru Mantra three times, the appearance is an illusion created by the devil. By the same principle, if one's personal deity appears in one's dream and disappears immediately after one recites the principal deity's mantra three times, the appearance is an illusion created by the devil.'
chapterbody 20: Question: 'Is the Vajrayana transmitted by Grandmaster Lu a transmission from Guru Padmasambhava?'
chapterbody 21: Answer: 'Yes.'
chapterbody 23: 'How was this Tantric Dharma transmitted?'
chapterbody 24: 'Long ago, Grandmaster Lu was King Trisong Detsen, who together with Guru Padmasambhava and Santarakshita established Samye Temple. Using the method of terma, Padmasambhava concealed the entire Vajrayana in the brain of Trisong Detsen.'
chapterbody 26: 'Terma transmission is formless. Is there also a tangible transmission?'
chapterbody 27: 'I have taken refuge in many gurus. The most important are Reverend Liaoming, Guru Sakya Zhengkong, the 16th Karmapa, Guru Thubten Dargye, and others.'
chapterbody 28: Note:
chapterbody 29: Reverend Liaoming—Great Mountain Jiji in Nantou.
chapterbody 30: Guru Sakya Zhengkong—Green Lake in Seattle, USA.
chapterbody 31: 16th Karmapa—New York State, USA.
chapterbody 32: Guru Thubten Dargye—Fu Yung Shan, Tsuen Wan, Hong Kong.
chapterbody 33: In addition:
chapterbody 34: Master Pufang—Shilin, Taipei, Taiwan.
chapterbody 36: Question: 'Is Guru Padmasambhava also a Padmakumara?'
chapterbody 37: Answer: 'Yes. Guru Padmasambhava appeared in a lotus blossom floating on Danakosha Lake. He emanated on a lotus and is a Padmakumara. At the same time, Padmasambhava is the body of Amitabha Buddha, the speech of Guanyin Bodhisattva, and the mind of Shakyamuni Buddha. He is the integration of these three.'
chapterbody 40: 'From Vairocana Buddha, Buddha Locana, Amitabha Buddha, and Padmakumara.'

chaptername: 044 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (4)
chapterbody: uestion: 'If one practices Guru Yoga, will one be reborn in the Western Pure Land?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'One will, of course, be reborn in the Western Pure Land. Amitabha Buddha emanated Padmakumara, who emanated Grandmaster Lu. One is reborn in the Western Pure Land because Grandmaster Lu is an emanation of Amitabha Buddha.'
chapterbody 4: 'In Vajrayana practice, there are, generally speaking, four major levels of empowerment, i.e. vase empowerment, secret empowerment, Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment, and Great Perfection empowerment. What is Grandmaster's viewpoint?'
chapterbody 5: 'In my view, cultivating according to the stages of empowerment is the safest and most reliable way to cultivate.'
chapterbody 6: Note:
chapterbody 7: I believe that vase empowerment includes cultivation of the Path of Accumulation, the Path of Preparation, and Guru Yoga. Secret empowerment is for cultivation of qi, channels, and light drops. Wisdom empowerment is for cultivation of Highest Yoga Tantra. Great Perfection empowerment is for cultivation of the absolute truth.
chapterbody 9: Question: 'In this book, Pith Teachings on the Path of Liberation, why have you not described cultivation of Highest Yoga Tantra?'
chapterbody 10: Answer: 'Highest Yoga Tantra can only be practiced by adepts and those with high capacity. For example, one must have seen the Path (emptiness) and have successfully accomplished Non-leakage Practice.
chapterbody 11: 'This means accomplishing Buddhahood in one's very body. Buddhahood in this lifetime and attainment of the rainbow body are hard to cultivate. Padmasambhava described these practices as being like snatching a pearl from the mouth of a poisonous snake.'
chapterbody 13: Question: 'Can you tell us a thing or two about this practice?'
chapterbody 14: Answer: 'One must first observe one's consort. Is the consort a 'Yogini'? This observation is divided into outer observation, inner observation, and observation of channels. It is forbidden to practice with a consort who has not attained yogic union.'
chapterbody 16: 'What about actual practice?'
chapterbody 17: 'Generally speaking, there are methods of cultivation and merit of accomplishment of three kinds of visualization, four kinds of skillful means, and the five cultivations of body, channels, qi, light drops, and emptiness bliss.'
chapterbody 18: Note:
chapterbody 19: Three kinds of visualization. (visualization of the personal deity, visualization of the secret area, great bliss visualization) Four kinds of skillful means. (descending, lifting, holding, dispersing) Five kinds of yoga. (vajra body, vajra channels, vajra qi, vajra light drops, vajra emptiness bliss) Consort technique and practice. (64, 16, 4) (not transmitted) Generation of merit. (Great bliss, light, emptiness) Attainment of rainbow body, etc.
chapterbody 21: Question: 'Do gods and ghosts know about this kind of practice?'
chapterbody 22: Answer: 'I say, transform the bed into a lotus. The consort and the practitioner are located in the same place as the pistil or stamens. The lotus closes and becomes a bud. This prevents gods and ghosts from knowing that the consort and the practitioner are practicing. They must perform boundary protection.'
chapterbody 24: 'How does the consort cultivate?'
chapterbody 25: 'The consort must observe whether the male practitioner is an adept. The consort's practice also includes the same three visualizations, four skillful means, and generation of merit.'
chapterbody 27: 'How can one tell if someone is falsely using the name 'consort practice' just to engage in sexual relations?'
chapterbody 28: 'Ask Grandmaster. I can see through it.'

chaptername: 045 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (5)
chapterbody: uestion: 'When choosing one's personal deity, what standard does one apply?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'A Vajrayana practitioner should choose whichever personal deity is congenial to the practitioner.'
chapterbody 4: 'Can one choose according to one's own personality?'
chapterbody 5: 'Yes. Of course.'
chapterbody 6: Note:
chapterbody 7: Compassionate—Guanyin Bodhisattva.
chapterbody 8: Wise—Manjusri Bodhisattva.
chapterbody 9: Full of Dharma power—Vajrapani Bodhisattva.
chapterbody 10: (These are just a few examples)
chapterbody 12: Question: 'How many emanations does Guanyin Bodhisattva have?'
chapterbody 13: Answer: 'Two arms, four arms, eight arms, 18 arms, 1,000 arms 1,000 eyes, the emanations of this bodhisattva are countless, even including Green Tara and White Tara, infinitely vast.'
chapterbody 15: 'Considering how many emanations there are, how should one choose a personal deity?'
chapterbody 16: 'If one is really unable to choose, one can consult Grandmaster Lu!'
chapterbody 18: Question: 'What is a peaceful deity? What is a wrathful deity? What is a peaceful and wrathful deity?'
chapterbody 19: Answer: 'The peaceful deities are civil and depicted in meditation. Wrathful deities are militaristic deities, depicted as subduing the four maras. Their image is wrathful. Peaceful and wrathful deities are half civil and half martial. Their image is half peaceful and half wrathful.'
chapterbody 21: 'Which of the above is important?'
chapterbody 22: 'They are all important.'
chapterbody 23: Note:
chapterbody 24: The Five Buddhas Transform into the Five Great Herukas.
chapterbody 25: The Eight Great Bodhisattvas transform into the Eight Great
chapterbody 26: Vidyarajas.
chapterbody 27: Also:
chapterbody 28: Kurukulla Buddha Mother is both peaceful and wrathful.
chapterbody 29: Vajravarahi is both peaceful and wrathful.
chapterbody 31: Question: 'All the deities have different personalities. Is this due to the need to subjugate the different personalities of practitioners?'
chapterbody 32: Answer: 'Yes. Yes. However, one must understand that just because a deity is compassionate, it doesn't mean the deity is lacking in wisdom. Or, that a deity who has wisdom is lacking in compassion. Also, wrathful deities are equally compassionate and equally have wisdom. Therefore, one can say that spiritual union with one dharma is spiritual union with all dharmas.'
chapterbody 34: Question: 'What is the difference between Amitabha Buddha, Infinite Light Buddha, and Infinite Life Buddha (Longevity Buddha)?'
chapterbody 35: Answer: 'There is really no difference at all. The three are one, one transformed into three. It is due to the needs of worldly people that they appear as separate transformations. This is true of all other deities as well.'
chapterbody 37: Question: 'In discoursing the Vajra dharma, which two deities does Grandmaster Lu consider most complete?'
chapterbody 41: 'My exposition of Lamdre is most complete, thus Hevajra is most complete. Guru Thubten Dargye transmitted the Kalachakra lineage to me, and this lineage was supplemented by the teachings of Gongtang Rinpoche.'

chaptername: 046 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (6)
chapterbody: uestion: 'Should we begin with personal deity practice or cultivation of the Vajra Dharma protector?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'Begin with personal deity practice, then cultivate the Vajra Dharma protector.'
chapterbody 3: 'Why?'
chapterbody 4: 'Because most Vidyarajas are emanations of the personal deity. After attaining spiritual union with the personal deity, one will be able to attain spiritual union with the Vidyaraja.'
chapterbody 6: Question: 'What is the origin of Vajrapani Bodhisattva?'
chapterbody 7: Answer: 'Mahasthamaprapta Bodhisattva.'
chapterbody 9: 'What is the origin of Yamantaka?'
chapterbody 10: 'Manjusri Bodhisattva.'
chapterbody 12: 'What is the origin of Mahachakra?'
chapterbody 13: 'Maitreya Bodhisattva.'
chapterbody 15: 'What is the origin of Hayagriva?'
chapterbody 16: 'Guanyin Bodhisattva.'
chapterbody 18: 'What is the origin of Mahottara?'
chapterbody 19: 'Adharma Buddha. (Adi Buddha, Primordial Buddha)'
chapterbody 21: 'What is the origin of Kalachakra?'
chapterbody 22: 'Shakyamuni Buddha.' (Emanated by the Buddha at Sri Dhanyakataka in Southern India)
chapterbody 23: Note:
chapterbody 24: The Buddhas of the Five Directions have five Vidyarajas, the Eight Great Bodhisattvas have eight Vidyarajas. Every bodhisattva has a Vidyaraja. A vidyaraja is a mind transformation of the buddhas and bodhisattvas.
chapterbody 26: Question: 'Ucchusma?'
chapterbody 27: Answer: 'Shakyamuni Buddha.'
chapterbody 29: 'Acala?'
chapterbody 30: 'An emanation of Mahavairocana.'
chapterbody 32: Question: 'What is the significance of a Vidyaraja?'
chapterbody 33: Answer: 'A Vidyaraja has tremendous majesty and divine powers, is able to vanquish all demons, eradicate disasters and difficulties due to earth, water, fire, and wind, protect the peace and tranquility of a practitioner's practice, annihilate black magic spells, eliminate malevolent harassment by ghosts, and entirely destroy all evil spirits''
chapterbody 35: 'Does True Buddha School have Dharma protectors? I heard there are two.'
chapterbody 39: 'Mahavairocana Buddha.' (Marici always rides in front of the sun)
chapterbody 42: 'Padmakumara.' (Mahabala is the integration of Padmakumara, Ksitigarbha, Mighty Envoy, Golden Mother, and Amitabha.)

chaptername: 047 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (7)
chapterbody: uestion: 'When learning Buddhism, generating bodhicitta is of utmost importance. What is meant by generating bodhicitta?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'Bodhicitta is focusing one's mind and heart on benefiting sentient beings. Great bodhicitta is regarding sentient beings as equivalent to oneself.'
chapterbody 3: Note:
chapterbody 4: Loving kindness—giving happiness to others.
chapterbody 5: Compassion—relieving the suffering of others.
chapterbody 6: Joy—offering to sentient beings in the ten directions and doing so with joy.
chapterbody 7: Equanimity—no difference between mind, buddha, and sentient beings. Equality.
chapterbody 9: Question: 'Why is generating bodhicitta of utmost importance?'
chapterbody 10: Answer: 'Where there is cause, there is effect. Generating bodhicitta is the cause. Attaining Buddhahood is the effect.'
chapterbody 12: 'When generating bodhicitta, is there a difference in the amount of bodhicitta generated?'
chapterbody 13: 'Yes, there is.'
chapterbody 14: Note:
chapterbody 15: Sravaka—deliver sentient beings after attaining personal liberation. (self-benefit)
chapterbody 16: Pratyekabuddha—liberate oneself first, hence the name solitary enlightened one. (self-benefit)
chapterbody 17: Bodhisattva—vow to attain Buddhahood after delivering sentient beings. (benefiting others)
chapterbody 18: Buddha—neither self nor others. Buddhas and sentient beings are equal. (equality of self and others)
chapterbody 20: Question: 'What is the meaning of 'Do no evil. Perform only good deeds. Purify one's mind. The teaching of the Buddha'?'
chapterbody 21: Answer: 'Doing no evil is to abide by the precepts. Performing all good deeds refers to bodhicitta. Purifying one's mind refers to cultivation. This is the Buddha's teaching.'
chapterbody 23: 'Could Grandmaster discourse the ultimate teaching?'
chapterbody 24: 'I say:
chapterbody 25: Thoughts are no thoughts.
chapterbody 26: Cultivation is no cultivation.
chapterbody 27: Attainment is no attainment.
chapterbody 28: Self is no self.
chapterbody 29: This is my ultimate teaching.'
chapterbody 30: (No self, no people, no sentient beings, no longevity)
chapterbody 32: Question: 'What offerings should one make to the personal deity?'
chapterbody 33: Answer: 'Flowers, incense, lamps, tea, and fruit are the five offerings. The eight offerings are these five offerings plus wish-fulfilling conch, solid perfume, and bathing water. There are actually a great many offerings, for example, the 37 offerings; Mt. Sumeru, Pravavideha, Jambudvipa Aparagodaniya, Uttarakuru, sun, moon, eight minor continents, wheel, elephant, horse, pearls, generals, innocent maidens, master of treasury, treasure, jewel mountains, oxen, jewel trees, rice, large canopy, small canopy, flowers, incense, lamps, solid perfume, joyful frolic, hair, singing, and dancing.'
chapterbody 35: 'So many?'
chapterbody 36: 'There are even more. These days, people give every kind of offering, for example, cars, houses, jewels, real estate, gold, silver, watches, etc. etc.'
chapterbody 38: 'Grandmaster, may we respectfully inquire what is the greatest offering?'
chapterbody 39: 'The offering of mind. The offering of Buddhadharma.'
chapterbody 42: 'Purity of body, purity of speech, and purity of mind are the greatest offerings. There is also the offering of great bliss, the offering of brilliant light, and the offering of emptiness. These are all number one offerings.' (Bodhicitta is a great offering)

chaptername: 048 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (8)
chapterbody: uestion: 'When practicing, should Vajrayana practitioners choose favorable geomancy?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'Yes. For example, the meditation caves of great practitioners, such as those of Padmasambhava and Milarepa, all have blessing power.'
chapterbody 3: Note:
chapterbody 4: Shakyamuni Buddha's Eight Holy Sites.
chapterbody 5: Mahakasyapa's cave.
chapterbody 6: Sariputra's cave.
chapterbody 7: Pindola's cave.
chapterbody 8: Avalokitesvara's Mt. Putuo.
chapterbody 9: Manjusri Bodhisattva's Mt. Wutai.
chapterbody 10: Samantabhadra Bodhisattva's Mt. Emei.
chapterbody 11: Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva's Mt. Jiuhua.
chapterbody 12: Also:
chapterbody 13: Seattle Ling Shen Ching Tze Temple in the US.
chapterbody 14: Lei Tsang Temple in Taiwan.
chapterbody 15: Seven Stars Landing of Rainbow Temple in the US, and so forth.
chapterbody 16: (These places all have spiritual energy)
chapterbody 18: Question: 'Were there any great practitioners who did not require enhancement by favorable geomancy?'
chapterbody 19: Answer: 'Yes. If a great practitioner has attained enlightenment, then 'I am the buddha'; I reside in a buddha shrine, my retinue are bodhisattvas, my clothes are pure celestial garments, everywhere I go is the pure land. This level of practitioner has a mind of equality, and can adapt anywhere.'
chapterbody 20: Note:
chapterbody 21: Slaughterhouse.
chapterbody 22: Brothel.
chapterbody 23: Continent of Rakshashas.
chapterbody 24: Continent of Yakshas.
chapterbody 25: Graveyard.
chapterbody 26: Wild forests.
chapterbody 27: Miasma sites.
chapterbody 28: So forth.
chapterbody 29: (Great practitioners can adapt to these places)
chapterbody 31: Question: 'Are there places where reciting mantras or the buddha name is prohibited? Such as a restroom or bathroom?'
chapterbody 32: Answer: 'Filth and purity are distinctions which exist in the mind of a sentient being; there is no such distinction of filth and purity in the mind of a holy sage. However, there are prohibitions on the mantras of some Dharma protectors because of the differences of their vows.'
chapterbody 34: Question: 'Must one who wants to recite any mantra take refuge in the guru?'
chapterbody 35: Answer: 'One is better off taking refuge, because one receives greater merit through the blessing power of the guru; there is less merit in reciting a mantra if one has not taken refuge.'
chapterbody 37: 'Is there a difference in the merit of reciting long, short, or heart mantras?'
chapterbody 38: 'There is no difference in merit. If one has time, one recites the long mantra; if one is pressed for time, one recites the short mantra.'
chapterbody 40: 'Grandmaster Lu, why do you specially revere the High King Sutra?'
chapterbody 41: 'Because its content is entirely composed of the names of buddhas and bodhisattvas. There are past buddhas, present buddhas, and future buddhas. There are also the names of great bodhisattvas. As taught in Buddhist scriptures, there is great merit in reciting the name of Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva.
chapterbody 42: 'The merit is, of course, tremendous for one who recites the names of buddhas and bodhisattvas of the ten directions and three times.'
chapterbody 45: 'The Six Syllable Mantra, the Great Compassion Dharani, the Manjusri Mantra, the Ushnishavijaya Mantra, the Seven Buddhas Mantra for Eradicating Transgressions. Also, the Padmakumara Mantra''

chaptername: 049 Dharma Words of Living Buddha Lian-sheng (9)
chapterbody: uestion: 'Grandmaster Lu! What is your view on religion?'
chapterbody 2: Answer: 'Equal! Equal! For example, I regard Jesus Christ as my guru. There is Buddhadharma within his teachings.'
chapterbody 3: Note:
chapterbody 4: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus transformed five loaves of bread and two fish into a feast for everyone present. This kind of great spiritual transformation is found in Buddhism as well.
chapterbody 5: Jesus said: Do not let the right hand know the good deeds the left hand is doing.
chapterbody 6: In the teachings of the Buddha, this is called 'don't think about your good deeds.' (Emptiness of the three wheels, no giver, no gift, no recipient)
chapterbody 7: Jesus said: If someone steals your coat, give him your undergarments too.
chapterbody 8: This is seeing oneself and sentient beings as one and the same. (Benevolence lacking a specific object and great compassion based on sameness in essence, unconditioned loving-kindness and compassion)
chapterbody 9: Jesus said: If someone slaps your right cheek, offer your left cheek.
chapterbody 10: This is the yoga of no-self. Jesus performed many miracles, such as walking upon the sea. He performed these miracles to teach his disciples profound faith and sincerity, to not have any doubts toward the guru.
chapterbody 12: Question: 'Besides Jesus, what do you think about other religions?'
chapterbody 13: Answer: 'Equality and reverence. I seek common ground. In the Hindu religion, I equally revere Mahabrahma, Vishnu, and Shiva.' 'Islam?' 'Revere.'
chapterbody 15: Question: 'Grandmaster Lu! Why do you pay such great homage to Jade Pond Golden Mother?'
chapterbody 16: Answer: 'Jade Pond Golden Mother transformed my life. She opened my divine eye, and guided me to the path of Buddhism; she is my great guru, my precious treasure. Everything I have in life was bestowed by her. She is the one I hold in highest reverence.'
chapterbody 18: Question: 'What is your opinion of Sutrayana and Vajrayana?'
chapterbody 19: Answer: 'They are harmonious, like the front and back of the hand.'
chapterbody 21: 'What if one practices both Sutrayana and Vajrayana?'
chapterbody 22: 'If thousands of people practice both Sutrayana and Vajrayana, these thousands of people will cross to the other shore!'
chapterbody 24: Question: 'Grandmaster Lu, why are your personal deities Jade Pond Golden Mother, Amitabha Buddha, and Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva?'
chapterbody 25: Answer:
chapterbody 26: 'Jade Pond Golden Mother—I pay her the highest reverence because she transformed my life.
chapterbody 27: Amitabha Buddha—He has the greatest vows, 48 vows to deliver sentient beings.
chapterbody 28: Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva—His profound vow, to not attain Buddhahood until the hells are empty.'
chapterbody 30: Question: 'Grandmaster Lu, what cultivation do you presently perform?'
chapterbody 31: Answer: 'I cultivate the Middle Way to purify my mind, seeing all dharmas as dreams and illusions while neither grasping nor abandoning them. Not abiding in any particular form while performing the Six Paramitas.'
chapterbody 33: Question: 'Do you enter samadhi?'
chapterbody 34: Answer: 'One-action samadhi, one-thought samadhi, one-form samadhi, no-thought samadhi, no-form samadhi, non-abiding samadhi. I study no-self, no-birth, no no-birth, no-extinction, emptiness, no-thought.' (the absolute truth on the path of liberation.)

chaptername: 50 Appendix:Teaching the Four Preliminaries
chapterbody: boarded an airplane to fly from Seattle, USA to Los Angeles, CA. The flight time was two hours and 40 min. Flying high above the clouds, the ground beneath the blue sky seemed very far away, and the cloud banks seemed to reach on and on endlessly.
chapterbody 2: I'm in the habit of sitting in the window seat so I can see outside and watch the myriad transformations of the cloud banks and the various cloud formations. Or, I may just read a book until I am tired, then close my eyes.
chapterbody 4: I had already performed a ritual as soon as I got on the plane, visualizing the personal deity above my crown, reciting the personal deity mantra, and meditating.
chapterbody 5: I believed it would be a smooth flight and a smooth landing. The plane would fly from the cloud banks above, through the clouds, gradually reaching the ground, and finally landing on the runway and coming to a stop.
chapterbody 7: The Los Angeles True Buddha School (TBS) masters, reverends, and disciples would be gathered at the arrival gate to welcome me with red cloth banners. There would be many disciples offering me flowers, presenting khatas, and many kneeling disciples waiting for blessings.
chapterbody 8: TBS disciples were not a noisy group. They lined up in two orderly rows, first offering flowers, then khatas, and finally red envelopes. At this time, I received the offerings with my left hand and gave crown blessings with my right hand.
chapterbody 10: According to my itinerary, I was to teach and bestow empowerment for the Four-Preliminaries Practice in Los Angeles. Every new disciple must first practice the Four Preliminaries, which is the most fundamental Vajrayana practice.
chapterbody 11: In the True Buddha Tantric Dharma, the Four Preliminaries are:
chapterbody 12: Great Homage Practice.
chapterbody 13: Mandala Offering Practice.
chapterbody 14: Fourfold Refuge Practice.
chapterbody 15: Vajra Heart Bodhisattva Practice.
chapterbody 17: I often say that new disciples should perform 100,000 sessions of the Great Homage, 100,000 sessions of the Mandala Offering, 100,000 recitations of the Fourfold Refuge Mantra, and perform the Vajrasattva Bodhisattva Practice until reaching spiritual union.
chapterbody 18: 'Spiritual union' is 'the spiritual sensation of enlightenment'.
chapterbody 20: Vajrayana cultivation must proceed by stages, gradually making progress level by level without skipping stages. When entering a new stage of cultivation for a higher Vajrayana dharma, prior to engaging in the practice, one must respectfully request empowerment for the dharma from the Root Guru or from masters. When cultivating a practice, one must attain 'spiritual union' in that practice prior to proceeding to the next higher level.
chapterbody 21: There is a saying in Vajrayana, 'Spiritual union in one practice is spiritual union in all practices.' Also, 'Attainment in one practice is attainment in all practices.' Therefore, we must not underestimate Four-Preliminaries Practice. To put it simply, if one does not attain spiritual union in the Four-Preliminaries Practice, it will be difficult to reach attainment in other practices. In other words, after attaining spiritual union in the Four-Preliminaries Practice, it will be easy to reach attainment in other practices.
chapterbody 23: As for Vajrayana practices, some are deep and some are relatively simple. Simple practices are like superficial vibration, whereas deep practices are like in-depth vibration. These simple to deep practices are similar to the Consciousness Only doctrine which starts from the consciousness of eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, then proceeds to manas consciousness, alaya consciousness, and to amala consciousness, which is the deepest consciousness.
chapterbody 24: The content of Vajrayana is so vast that it encompasses the entirety of all realms. Its cultivation of external actions impacts the internal consciousness. Although the deep level of consciousness hidden inside is important, its importance is still on the surface. One can say that inner and outer are one and the same, connected and indivisible.
chapterbody 25: The Great Homage, Mandala Offering, Fourfold Refuge, and Vajra Heart Practice are skilfully and ingeniously integrated. Within the scheme of simple preliminaries, a deep level is established. With later development and growth, upon attainment of spiritual union, one enters the supreme wisdom of the Tathagata.
chapterbody 27: The Four-Preliminaries Practice is not at all superficial. Within this practice, the Vajra Heart Practice and its 100 Syllable Mantra are the most amazing and wonderful dharmas in Vajrayana. The 100 Syllable Mantra is equivalent to the Heart Sutra. The principal deity of Vajra Heart Practice is Vajrapani Bodhisattva, who is also Vajrasattva, the lineage guru of Vajrayana and the Dharma Prince of the Five Tathagatas of the Five Directions. From spiritual union attained through Vajra Heart Practice, an infinite and ceaseless Dharma flow will be produced. This will lead to deep spiritual union and absolute realization.
chapterbody 28: I say,
chapterbody 29: 'The deepest level will only be attained through actual practice.'
chapterbody 30: 'One drop of water is an ocean.'
chapterbody 31: 'One mote of dust is a mountain.'
chapterbody 33: We frequently give this example. The Four Preliminaries are the vajra foundation on which one builds a vajra jeweled stupa. Without the firm stability of the vajra foundation, how can there ever be a vajra jeweled stupa? Fortified with Four-Preliminaries Practice one can definitely establish a jeweled stupa!
chapterbody 34: Cultivate the Vajrayana without hurry, without confusion, without skipping stages. With solid practice, one will receive spiritual union. A great practitioner will definitely practice like this.
chapterbody 35: This chapter is excerpted from H.H. Living Buddha Lian-sheng, Sheng-Yen Lu's book 123, Shooting Stars and Red Maples.

chaptername: 051 Appendix: Four-Preliminaries Practice Is a Major Practice
chapterbody: frequently tell everyone, 'Four-Preliminaries Practice is a significant and major practice.' Some disciples are doubtful, 'Four-Preliminaries Practice is the most fundamental practice of Vajrayana, the foundation. How can it be a major practice?'
chapterbody 2: Please listen to these words from my innermost heart:
chapterbody 3: 'Without the foundation, it's impossible to attain the next level. Therefore, Four-Preliminaries Practice is a practice of major significance.'
chapterbody 4: Great Homage—subjugation of one's arrogance.
chapterbody 5: Fourfold Refuge—generation of bodhicitta to eradicate karmic hindrances.
chapterbody 6: Mandala Offering—accumulation of provisions.
chapterbody 7: Vajrasattva Practice—purification, repentance of transgressions.
chapterbody 8: One must understand that all Vajrayana practices, such as Guru Yoga, personal deity practice, vajra practices, Highest Yoga Tantra, the Great Perfection, Mahamudra, Lamdre, and Yamantaka, regardless of the practice, are not separate from the Four-Preliminaries Practice.
chapterbody 9: www.yifupublications.com
chapterbody 10: I will say this one sentence to everyone in all honesty:
chapterbody 11: 'With utmost sincerity, one will attain esoteric fruition.'
chapterbody 12: These words apply to both Sutrayana and Vajrayana.
chapterbody 13: 129 Entering the Most Hidden Yin-Yang Realm
chapterbody 14: In Sutrayana, it is said, 'Through single-minded concentration, one attains rebirth in the pure land.'
chapterbody 15: In Vajrayana, it is said, 'With utmost sincerity, one will attain esoteric fruition.'
chapterbody 16: Books
chapterbody 17: You should know that the original name of the Mahavairocana Sutra [Mahavairocana Tantra] was the Mahavairocana Perfect Enlightenment Transformation Blessing Sutra [Mahavairocana Abhisabodhi Vikurvita Adhihana Tantra]. The key point is 'blessing spiritual transformation into Buddhahood'.
chapterbody 18: When a Vajrayana practitioner attains purification of body, speech, and mind through cultivation of the Four-Preliminaries Practice, the three secrets of the Tathagata merge with the three karmas of the sentient being. When the three secrets and the three karmas are spiritually united and fuse into oneness, one is blessed and transformed to Buddhahood.
chapterbody 19: 145 Living this Moment in Purity
chapterbody 20: The incredible thing about Vajrayana is that there is no difference between the Dharma-body buddha and an ordinary person. This is the secret secret secret secret secret secret.
chapterbody 21: Great Homage (purification of body).
chapterbody 22: Fourfold Refuge, Mandala Offering (purification of speech)
chapterbody 23: Vajra Heart Practice (purification of mind)
chapterbody 24: Isn't this so?
chapterbody 25: Upon self purification of the three karmas, one receives the blessings of the Tathagata resulting in spiritual transformation into Buddhahood. With this blessing, one accomplishes the vast great perfection, great unhindered compassion, helping people in distress, longevity, no birth and no death, wish fulfillment, liberation, and rebirth in the pure land.

chapterbody 27: Vajrayana mantras can hold, can shield, and are all-inclusive.
chapterbody 28: Four-Preliminaries Practice is the foundation of Vajrayana. When the foundation is firm and stable, one can quickly transcend to the pure land and attain Buddhahood through the blessing of spiritual transformation. How is this not a major practice?
